data.title: Agnico Eagle: Top-Quality Miner Built For A New Gold Reality
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), I highlighted their top-tier quality and an even stronger position following the increase in gold prices, capitalizing on the jump seen throughout the year, reiterating their Buy rating.

With an exceptional report, updated medium-term guidance, and the achievement of several records, yet still a conservative valuation given gold's potential and current prices, Agnico remains a very solid option to consider for anyone interested in gold, remaining a Buy despite their surge.

Internal Developments

Agnico reported a stellar Q4 and 2025, beating the market's top- and bottom-line estimates with records in virtually everything, balance sheet improvements, and an AISC of only $1,339 per ounce throughout the year, which further proves the company's quality alongside their long mine life and quality.

As for the cash flow, we can see a very strong $4.4 billion reported in 2025 (more than twice the $2.14 billion seen in 2024), and that's with an average realized gold price of $3,454 per ounce. As a note, another advantage here is that Agnico doesn't have to worry about older hedges, being able to fully enjoy the rise in gold prices.

To get an idea, if we assume a 45% increase in their operating cash flow as a result of a ~$5,000 average gold price and the same CAPEX in 2026, we're talking about a free cash flow of nearly $7.5 billion in 2026, which is not bad at all for a $107.76 billion market cap, especially given their growth potential, but that will ultimately depend on gold prices, of course.

As AEM highlights, this is the strongest position in their history - which makes sense given the price of gold - which fuels their 20% to 30% potential organic growth (and more if we're looking beyond organic) over the next decade, while their reserves also reached a new record alongside the cash flow, which is very solid given their quality.

The company also highlighted their solid next phase of growth, backed by several projects that can ramp up from 2030, adding significant high-quality production.

The company also provided an updated three-year guidance, hinting at "stable production at peer-leading costs," expecting 3.3 to 3.5 million ounces of production in 2026, at an AISC between $1,400 and $1,550 per ounce and spending between $2.47 billion and $2.73 billion in total, plus potential for another ~$300 million to $350 million depending on the Hope Bay construction decision that can come in Q2.

Financially, based on AEM's latest report, we can see a very strong position, with their current assets covering their current liabilities very well and nearly no debt left, as they repaid about $1 billion worth of debt while also building a very strong cash position throughout the year that can help them continue their projects.

AEM also approved a 12.5% quarterly dividend increase, getting to $0.45 per share, for a ~0.83% yield based on today's price, although I wouldn't expect excellent yields on cost from these levels.

Macro Developments

Of course, by far the biggest development since last covering them has been the massive surge in gold prices, which briefly got to a record of about $5,600/oz before falling to ~$5,000 now, which is still a monumental improvement compared to the previous "high" of less than half this price that we saw half a decade ago.

I'll mention again that this surge should not be such a big surprise, as gold's potential upside is theoretically not only unlimited since it's not really tied to some elastic "real" uses (as I'll show below), but it also comes after the market has ignored the fundamental need for it for so long.

While the market's focus has been on the pandemic, stay-at-home bubble, and now AI stocks, I'd argue that it has largely ignored the need for a hedge, only recently "waking up" in a very unpredictable and volatile environment without being properly hedged, and this surge in gold is more like a massive amount of pent-up demand that built over the past half a decade or so, continuing the pre-pandemic rise in a rapid burst instead of a long-term increase.

This wake-up call was likely accelerated by all the headlines seen in recent months, from the US Government going through its longest shutdown in history, the country's debt held by the public reaching 100% of the GDP for the first time leading to crisis worries, Japan's economic plans threatening the USD and treasuries, tariffs, and, of course, rate cuts and the effects of all the money-printing seen during the pandemic.

Plus, these recent factors come in what's already a weak and volatile environment, with fear around the consumer trade-downs even for the most basic necessities such as food, weak earnings reports from major companies for years (especially consumer-related), AI overspending, and many other headwinds for the economy that can actually benefit gold for longer, or at the very least lead to a "new reality" for gold prices going forward.

As we can see, this jump can easily be explained by the massive surge in demand, especially from investing, as even though the demand from central banks and other institutions dropped by 37% in Q4'25 vs. Q4'24, the demand coming from investment more than offset it, with a whopping 84% increase in 2025 compared to 2024, while we've also seen an emergence in gold ETFs (up 756% in Q4 compared to virtually nothing in 2024).

As mentioned above, although small as a percentage of the total, the demand from technology has been extremely resilient, basically remaining flat despite the jump in gold prices, while jewelry has also been relatively resilient, only dropping by ~19% in 2025, proving its inelastic demand that I highlighted before, as a higher gold price also means higher value.

Agnico's CEO also talked about the company's approach to M&As and capital allocation during their Q4 Earnings Call, stating that they are open to taking advantage of opportunities when they may arise, as long as they are accretive on a per-share basis:

(...) we are willing to move and we have moved when we see an opportunity on the M&A side that actually creates value per share. We're not interested in just getting bigger. The hard part is actually creating value per share. And so that's going to always be the driver, not only of M&A, but all of our capital allocation decisions.

I'd add that this is not the only thing they're looking for, as Agnico is technically able to buy almost anything they want using stock and deliver accretive per-share results given their valuation premium, but I think they also define value as a quality- and risk-adjusted factor, as they could've already bought plenty of mines in risky jurisdictions if they just wanted mathematical value per share.

Still, knowing that they can use this premium valuation to acquire peers - the quickest example is probably Alamos (AGI) if they want to add another strong, healthy, and high-quality portfolio under their name - is ultimately an advantage that's defining them and has been core for Agnico for so long. Note that I'm talking about "flashier" acquisitions, not small stakes acquired in other miners/projects, which they already do, such as selling Gunnarn Mining AB and buying more of Goldsky Resources recently.

Meanwhile, we can see AEM continuing to be strong in terms of AISC compared to their peers, as the latest data (as of Q3'25) shows a continued increase in AISC, reaching over $1,500 per ounce recently as a result of a mix between inflation (workforce, fuel, electricity, equipment, supplies, etc.) combined with improved feasibility of what would've otherwise not been worth it or offered an incentive that's too small.

Note, however, that this indicator is relevant mostly for the companies themselves, as the price of gold has little to do with the supply, similar to how silver has been running on a supply deficit for about half a decade while the price stagnated as well.

The price of gold is much more than just a factor of AISC and supply-demand, especially in such a "financially engineered" commodity where the demand from various financial instruments (see the Investment section in the image above) ultimately makes the price.

As a result of all the contracts that have little to do with the actual supply, it is significantly less relevant compared to most other commodities, which are tied to "real" consumption and production, but that also makes gold's potential nearly boundless.

Risks Reiterated

However, that also means that the price can be affected significantly if this demand drops, and even though I don't see many reasons for that to happen, it's certainly a possibility that will have significant effects on AEM's valuation.

Meanwhile, the company's unhedged exposure to gold can also come back to bite them, as the same way they enjoyed the ride so far, a potential crash can also hurt them just as bad, which can slow down their developments while near-term production is expected to remain stable.

Valuation

For AEM's DCF valuation, I'll go for an enterprise value one to take into account their solid cash position. As a result, I'll start with their operating cash flow of $6.82 billion, minus the $2.42 billion in CAPEX, plus a ~$67 million after-tax interest, and we get to an FCFF of about $4.47 billion in 2025.

In 2026, if we assume a relatively conservative average gold price of $4,250 per ounce, that should be a rough increase of ~23% to their OCF, meaning $8.39 billion, minus $2.6 billion in CAPEX based on the midpoint of their guidance, meaning about $5.8 billion in FCFF in 2026 if we assume a slight boost thanks to more interest income. I'll assume they can grow this at a CAGR of 3% until 2030 thanks to interest income and other slight improvements, followed by a 20% boost from 2031 and another 5% from 2033, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate a WACC of 9% this time to account for a better margin of safety, with $2.67 billion in net cash and 502.73 million shares outstanding as of the Q4 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $102.01 billion to an equity value of $104.68 billion, meaning about $208.23 per share, which is almost identical to the current $216.59, which is a sign that the market is approaching this very carefully.

Conclusion

I reiterate AEM's Buy rating, as the company offers a solid valuation, excellent financial position, and some of the best quality we can find in gold mining.

There is certainly risk coming from gold, as that will ultimately be the key factor defining this company's price, but the longer these prices remain high, the better it is, and I believe AEM remains a strong contender to consider for anyone that believes in gold's potential.

data.title: TSI: Massive 30% Dividend Raise For This Monthly Income Fund
data.description:
Overview

TCW Strategic Income Fund (TSI) operates as a closed-end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of income-producing assets. As market indices continue to experience volatility and uncertainty driven by the tech sector, TSI can offer an income-focused hedge that preserves capital. Since the fund doesn't have exposure to equities, the share price is disconnected from what's happening to the indices. The fund puts an emphasis on income generation over time, which comes with its own set of benefits and vulnerabilities.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that TSI's share price has fallen by about 2.9%. The fund has failed to hold onto the positive growth momentum of the market over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 4.6% over the same time frame. TSI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 6.9% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. After reviewing the fund's reporting metrics, I believe that TSI is capable of keeping its distributions consistent over the next 12 months.

While TSI has proven to be a great income position, there are still some risks that should be considered. For instance, the exposure to debt securities introduces some interest rate sensitivity over a longer holding period, which means that near-term performance can remain limited if interest rates stay elevated. Additionally, the focus on income generation can result in severe underperformance, so this fund only makes sense for investors that seek safety over total return. So, let's start by taking a look at the strategy that TSI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, TSI has total managed assets of $247.6M that are spread across 682 different holdings. The fund has an objective of providing attractive total returns with an emphasis on high current income. In order to achieve this goal, the fund invests in a blend of different income-producing securities across different industries. So instead of investing in equities and relying on the earnings growth of its positions, the fund's focus on debt securities means that TSI is betting on a borrower's ability to repay its loans.

Looking at the portfolio breakdown, we can see that TSI is most heavily allocated towards corporate securities at 26.1% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities and non-agency mortgage-backed securities, which accounts for 23.4% and 23.7% of assets, respectively. Since the combination of these three asset types makes up more than 73% of total assets, investors should anticipate that these will have the greatest impact on TSI's daily price movement and total return potential.

While it isn't specifically highlighted in the fund's documents, I would assume that a significant portion of TSI's assets are structured on a floating rate basis. Therefore, TSI may be able to benefit from this high interest rate environment and collect more income from its positions. A high interest rate directly translates to higher interest income that can be collected from borrowers. However, a high cost of debt can also suppress the valuation of these securities.

To offset the risks associated with a higher interest rate environment, the majority of TSI's assets are allocated to securities with strong credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating above BBB are classified as investment grade and have the lowest risk of being impacted by defaults. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 69.7%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 37.7%
  • Not Rated: 2.7%

Valuation & Performance Risk

Since TSI is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, TSI now trades at a discount to NAV of 7.62%. For reference, TSI has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.28% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund now trades at one of the most attractive price-to-NAV discount levels over the last five years. While this can be an indication that it can be a solid time to add, I wanted to review the most updated report that is available to shareholders.

The semi-annual report for 2025 is currently the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.15 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.10 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that TSI is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with prior years. This is an indication that the fund's portfolio strategy is efficient at generating income through different market cycles. However, the prior years also reveal that net investment income levels do not always outpace the distributions being paid. Therefore, TSI is sometimes reliant on net realized gains to bridge that gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary since they are directly influenced by the momentum of the market. For instance, TSI experienced a large net realized loss of $0.69 per share in 2022 and still hasn't generated net realized gains in any of the following years that have offset this prior loss. So periods of large declines can drastically impact the NAV growth that is experienced over time. As it currently stands, TSI's NAV still sits below its pre-pandemic level. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $5.85 per share
  • 2021: $5.69 per share
  • 2022: $4.94 per share
  • 2023: $5.05 per share
  • 2024: $5.03 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $5.15 per share

Since TSI has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions and pays out most of its earnings as distributions, this results in limited price growth over a longer holding period. For instance, TSI's share price has declined by 7.8% over the last 10-year period, despite market indices racing to new all-time highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 72.1% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns may come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

As part of the fund's operating strategy, it may also deploy leverage up to 33% of total assets in an effort to amplify its earnings. This is an aggressive use of leverage during this high interest rate environment, which can actually increase the overall risk profile. Leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. In a higher interest rate environment, the cost of debt can actually negatively impact TSI's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. This will actively suppress the share price until interest rates trend lower and improve the fund's operating spread.

Although the Fed has initiated several 25 basis point cuts throughout 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. As we can see below, TSI's share price remains far below its pre-pandemic range. Therefore, it may actually be a great time for patient investors to accumulate at these levels if you are optimistic that lower interest rates will eventually serve as a catalyst for TSI's share price. A lower cost of debt can attract new borrowers in the market, which management can capitalize on and grow their base assets over time.

Lastly, investors should limit their expectation on the fund's ability to provide resilience through severe downturns. If the fund sees large net realized losses, TSI's share price will also decline alongside market indices. TSI may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earned, which will cause the NAV to erode. While distributions can help offset losses, the actual share price may still see larger losses than a traditional index-tracking ETF, such as the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). As we can see below, SPY saw a total loss of 18.17% through 2022's decline. While TSI's total return was slightly lower at 14.31%, the share price still suffered from a greater decline through the year.

Dividend Raise Is Supported

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0283 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.9%. This latest declared distribution also represents a massive 30.4% raise, which can be an indication of management's confidence about future earnings. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.3396 per share. Referring to the same semi-annual report, the fund has an inconsistent coverage history, but I still believe that TSI will be able to sustain payouts over the next twelve months. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.40 per share
  • 2021: $0.21 per share
  • 2022: ($0.43) per share
  • 2023: $0.47 per share
  • 2024: $0.37 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.25 per share

As we can see, the fund has no issues generating earnings that can outpace the estimated annual payout amount. Therefore, TSI can be a great choice for investors that seek a source of reliable monthly income. Assuming that TSI is capable of capturing a similar earnings amount over the remainder of the 2025 period, the fund should have produced enough excess to capture some NAV growth and may lead to a supplemental distribution in the future.

Estimated 2025 earnings of ~$0.50 per share / $0.3396 annual payout = 1.47 years of coverage.

However, it's important for investors to consider the fact that all distributions received from TSI may not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the last payout was classified as net investment income, which is considered ordinary dividends and has the greatest tax consequence for investors. Therefore, a position in TSI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that TSI is a buy at its current price-to-NAV valuation. After reviewing the fund's reporting, I believe that TSI is aligned to continue generating earnings that can support the distributions being paid. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, I believe that there's a good chance TSI's share price will eventually recover back to its pre-pandemic levels. A lower cost of debt may be a growth catalyst since it will improve operating spreads and reduce the burden of leverage. Historically, the fund has done a great job at preserving capital over a long period of time, and I believe that patient investors will benefit from accumulation.

data.title: Landstar System: The Selloff Is Valid, But It's Still Fully Priced
data.description:
It has only been two months since my previous coverage of Landstar System, Inc. (LSTR). And yet, we have already seen notable changes, which justifies my downgrade from strong buy to buy. True to the title of my previous article, LSTR really did reach my target price of $155.69 and even exceeded it before selling pressures took over and dragged it down to $142.69. I can’t blame the market due to the existing structural industry challenges, macroeconomic volatility, and even AI. Despite this, LSTR’s fundamentals and operational efficiency ensure its resilience and durability. Nonetheless, caution has become more logical as valuation now shows limited upside. This is in line with the technicals that show early bearish tendencies, although buying opportunities are present.

LSTR Q4 2025: Challenges Persist

Even now, macroeconomic headwinds are still visible amid the rising cost pressures and softer demand. Freight and logistics are among the first to experience the blow due to their changing rates and volumes/loads. Landstar System, Inc. is no exception as its lackluster growth continues. Despite this, its asset-light and well-diversified business model provides it with some insulation. But of course, this was not enough to compensate for the weaker market dynamics.

During Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.17B, down by 2.9% YoY from $1.21B. Up to this point, LSTR still does not indicate a strong rebound anytime soon. If you remember, the YoY revenue increase in my previous coverage was 0.7%. Weaker demand and pricing power in platform truckload, ocean cargo, and other truck carriers were evident. Stubborn inflation and tariff woes still had an effect on the market, so it's not surprising that LSTR was also affected despite some nuances in its business model. On a lighter note, its LTL rate and loads increased, suggesting that it continued to benefit from the LTL market undercapacity. Rail intermodal also had some strengths. More interestingly, its revenues and loads via BCO independent contractors still increased.

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses decreased, which shows its efforts to improve efficiency. It can also imply prudent fleet management, supported by the lower fuel prices. This is evident in its purchased transportation and commissions. The only problem was the huge increase in insurance and claims, which remained rampant in the industry. Overall, its actual performance weakened, but cost-management strategies helped it maintain positive profits.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation is still a big risk factor for LSTR. At 2.7%, this is still close to the 14-month high of 3.0%. Prices are still increasing, and so are cost pressures. LSTR may also have to raise employee wages and driver insurance to help them cope with the rising prices. This may be exacerbated by new tariffs since they raise the domestic production costs, raising prices further. With that, purchasing power and pricing flexibility across industries may weaken. The volume of goods produced and transported may also be affected. That is why the number of loads in many of its transportation platforms decreased.

AI fear trade also appears to be sweeping the market. Concerns about artificial intelligence potentially disrupting the market are spreading. Although those are still speculative, the reaction is huge and sharp. And I think that this was due to the idea that investors are cutting old economy and shifting to pure AI or tech plays. With that, trucking companies that are investing in AI and other modern technologies may support their upside. In response, LSTR may also have to scale up its investment here since AI is expected to drive efficiency through better route optimization and timely road traffic updates.

Mitigants

Despite these challenges, LSTR still has some strengths to insulate its business. Its business model is already an insulation. I’ve already explained this one repeatedly. Its asset-light and well-diversified business model allows it to spread risks, manage its cost sensitivity, and lower capital intensity. And because BCOs are the ones that provide drivers, their exposure to driver shortages and higher insurance risks is not as high as many market players.

Liquidity is another important strength of the company. Its cash and cash equivalents are still adequate at $396.7M. If we include short-term investments, the total will be $452.2M. This is equivalent to 28% of the total assets, which makes it very liquid. In addition, this is more than enough to cover the total debt. With that, it still has a huge capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity. More importantly, it may continue paying regular and special dividends.

With respect to the LTL market, LSTR still has some opportunities to take advantage of. The market still experiences undercapacity due to driver shortages and the bankruptcy of many players in 2023-2025. Note that this wasn’t limited to small players. Names like Dolche Truckload, Daniel Trucking International, Inc., and Elite Carriers were on the list. In fact, there were approximately 10,000 carriers that closed their doors just in H1 2024. With that, the stronger ones who remain in the market capture more demand. And because e-commerce and smaller packages are still resilient, the LTL market benefits from these trends. It’s not surprising that the LTL PPI is soaring to its 10-year high of 268.92. In line with that, LSTR sets higher LTL rates.

Lastly, it may benefit from the policy easing cycle. This can help businesses and consumers cope with inflation and tariffs. Because of lower borrowing costs, production and consumption will stay resilient, and so will freight and logistics. Additionally, it may help LSTR manage liquidity and profitability better, since lower interest rates can also lower the repricing of its variable-rate borrowings.

Valuation

The stock price drop of LSTR is valid if you relate it to the TP, or target price, in my previous analysis. It has already exceeded my TP. As an investor here, I decided to lighten my share here. Of course, its softer performance is another factor. And as I look into price ratios, I also understand that LSTR is still fully priced.

Using P/E, the stock is now trading at 43.01x. This is so high if you ask me. It is also higher than the five-year average of 25.51. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $84.43. Of course, this is not conclusive due to several factors. First, the market was highly volatile during the pandemic years of 2020-2022. In 2023-2024, the freight recession began. Since 2025, the market has been attempting to normalize, but weakness is still evident. But more or less, LSTR is fully priced. Meanwhile, P/S shows that the stock is still reasonable. However, it also appears fully priced since the TP is $142.78.

Lastly, P/B suggests a TP of $149.47. If you look at the trend of BVPS in 2021-2025, you can say that the price has been most reactive and consistent with it. The stock increased when BVPS increased, and vice versa. Nonetheless, the gap between the TP and the current price is still narrow. So, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

LSTR shows bearish tendencies due to the huge selloff last Thursday. It broke below the first two key support levels. Now, it’s trading below the 50-day SMA, which suggests that the current momentum is weaker than the average in the past 50 days.

Despite this, the possibility of a retraction and a consolidation should not be ignored since it still moves above the 200-day SMA. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, so market sentiments are still on the positive side.

Its MACD and RSI support this. The MACD line is decreasing, while the histogram is within negative territory, which shows that the selloff exceeded buying volume. However, this has already led to overselling, which may prompt investors to take advantage of the huge dip

In the end, these contrasting indicators warrant some caution as we weigh their impact with fundamentals and valuation.

Conclusion

Landstar System, Inc. remains resilient despite the big headwinds it faces. Its strategic business model and liquidity provide some insulation as it takes advantage of some LTL market opportunities. However, valuation warrants some caution as the stock appears fully priced. Technicals adhere to it as bearish tendencies remain evident, although the overselling may open new entry opportunities. I’m downgrading my rating from buy to hold.

data.title: Tenaris: 'The Coiling Spring' Of The Energy Patch
data.description:
Tenaris: A Proxy for Rigs

Tenaris (TS, TNRSF) is probably one of the most underfollowed companies that plays a global role in a key industry. In fact, as I already explained when I initiated my coverage of the company, it is one of the leading manufacturers of steel pipes for the oil and gas industry. It specializes in oil country tubular goods (OCTG), and it is also at the forefront of offshore and onshore pipeline manufacturing technology. It is truly spread worldwide, with manufacturing, R&D, and service centers on every continent. I

It has a well-diversified customer base since it serves most of the large oil companies. At first glance, we could think that Tenaris is just a proxy for steel prices. But this is far from the truth since Tenaris' products belong to the premium category and come together with the company's technological know-how, its logistics support, and its execution. Being exposed to upstream companies, Tenaris is cyclical. Simply said, when oil and gas operators drill, Tenaris sells more pipes, and its margins improve. When drilling decelerates, so do Tenaris' sales, and its margins compress. So, we could say that Tenaris is perhaps a proxy for active rigs. As we can see, the latest analysis that the EIA released shows that the most recent rig peak was in December 2022. Right now, we are seeing mildly declining activity, which makes some analysts believe that we could be close to a trough.

At the same time, the latest U.S. crude oil production estimates that we will continue seeing output rates near the 2025 record of 13.6 million b/d.

So, even though rigs are fewer, oil production is near record levels because the industry has achieved outstanding improvements when it comes to drilling efficiency. Lateral wells, for example, are a big game-changer because they stem horizontally from one vertically drilled well. In the Permian, for example, rigs declined by 29% between 2022 and 2025, while oil production actually increased by 18%.

Since Tenaris uses steel, it is exposed to steel tariffs. However, its manufacturing footprint has meaningful U.S. exposure, and this surely helps. Here, the World Steel Association projects that in 2026 there will be a "modest rebound of 1.3%" in global steel demand, driven by a rebound in the U.S., Europe, and the Middle East. Steel is clearly an input that Tenaris uses. Since Tenaris is vertically integrated, that is, it can produce its own steel that it then turns into pipes, when steel prices (hot-rolled sheet/strip) increase, Tenaris has an advantage over the competitors that need to buy steel on the open market. This is also true with steel scrap, which Tenaris also produces internally. As we can see, scrap prices have come down from the 2022 peak, but they are still moderately elevated. As we will see in a moment, Tenaris has been able to keep its EBITDA margin high. In addition, since Tenaris produces steel also within the U.S., it is protected from import tariffs and from competitive importers from China and other countries.

Tenaris is also exposed to Brent/WTI prices because they influence the budgets of Tenaris' customers. Usually, when WTI stays between $65 and $75 per barrel, Tenaris is able to report EBITDA margins of 24%. If oil prices move down below the breakeven point (for the Permian, it is between $45 and $55), there could be another decline in rig count, which could pressure Tenaris' business and reduce its margins to around 20%, which, in any case, is still very healthy. Conversely, if oil moves above $85/barrel, Tenaris will see growing volumes and will benefit from operating leverage.

Tenaris' Main Financials

We will soon read Tenaris' FY25 report. However, Tenaris said during the Q3 earnings call that it expects stable sales and an EBITDA margin between 20% and 25%. This means that we expect revenues of $2.85B, which means that it could end the fiscal year with revenues between $11.8B and $12B (the consensus is $11.9B).

The company is known for keeping its SG&A always under control at around 14% of revenues. Interest expense is almost nonexistent, being less than $10M.

After all, Tenaris' balance sheet is nothing but superb. It holds $3B in cash and cash equivalents, and its total debt is $478M. Tenaris issues debt, but it has been repaying more than it issues (TTM debt issuance: $917M, TTM debt repayment: $1.11B). Its inventory is always stable around $3.5B, which is quite impressive for an industrial company that is exposed to some cyclicality. As a result of this solidity, Tenaris has been able to buy back shares aggressively: since late 2023, the share count is down by 11%, and this doesn't yet reflect what happened in Q4, when share repurchases continued. On top of this, Tenaris pays a nice 3.6% dividend, paid semiannually in May and November.

Tenaris is able to generate around FCF between $1.7B and $2B per year, with strong years that can achieve $2.2B. In a situation where interest expense is minuscule and capex is steady at around $650M per year, we have a perfect setup to jump onboard during the softer part of the cycle to expect FCF growth and larger shareholder returns when Tenaris' cash position explodes.

Since Tenaris has almost no debt, its key financial metric remains EBITDA because it directly influences the trajectory of its EPS. This means that we need to focus on its volumes. While we expect North America to remain more or less flat, as rig activity is softening, international volumes are usually linked to long-cycle projects, which are more difficult to turn on and off as fast as it can be done with shale. Currently, big offshore projects in Guyana, Brazil, and the Middle East could compensate for U.S. rig activity deceleration. Since Tenaris has around 40% of its business in North America but the remaining 60% around the world, we have a balanced mix, which makes me think that a little bit of growth will happen in 2026. Surely, we won't see its EBITDA margin above 25%, which would be an extremely bullish target for Tenaris. But neither will we see it below 18%, which would probably trigger a rerating.

Tenaris' Valuation

Well, even though Tenaris now trades at $48, which is an all-time high, its fwd PE is extremely interesting: 13.6. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is 7.6, while its P/FCF is a bit more expensive and stands at 13.6. The real drag to this interesting setup is that Tenaris' growth forecast for 2026 is not great, and many analysts believe that we will still see some margin compression and an EBITDA decline.

However, these numbers will soon change once Tenaris reports FY25 and initiates its 2026 guidance. In fact, they are currently based on the 2025 estimates. But once the fwd looking metrics roll over to 2026, we will be talking about a company that could report $12.2B in revenues (the consensus is $12B) and $2.1B in net income. In fact, from what we are seeing, 2026 is not going to be a bad year for Tenaris, and the company might report some growth and even some margin expansion. If this is the case, let's imagine what the PEG ratio could be. The fwd PE is 13.6, and 2026 EPS could grow 3% YoY. This would lead to a 4.5 PEG ratio, which is expensive. But if margins do expand and we also factor in the positive effect of the buybacks ($1.2B, which is equal to 5% of the current market cap), we could expect the company to end the year with 6% or 7% EPS growth, which would compress the PEG ratio to around 2. Still a bit expensive, but we would need to consider that Tenaris is going through a trough and a new peak will come after 2026.

However, for cyclical companies, the PEG ratio is not the best metric because growth rates swing. This is why I want to add the EV/EBITDA perspective, split into three scenarios. We always use the forecast of revenues near $12.2B, then we use three different EBITDA margins, which give three different values for EBITDA. A very cheap multiple would be below 6. In this case, we don't see it, even in the bull case scenario. So, we could say that the stock is fairly priced right now. By the way, a fair price means a fair deal, which is different from an expensive deal.

Scenario Revenue 26E EBITDA Margin EBITDA 26E EV/EBITDA
Bear $12.2 B 19.0% $2.32 B 9.2x
Base $12.2 B 21.5% $2.62 B 8.2x
Bull $12.2 B 24.0% $2.93 B 7.3x

However, I think the bull case for Tenaris is real because we are in a phase of the cycle where the company is preparing for the next peak. We could say it is a coiling spring. This is why I think the stock should be on our watchlist, especially if we are understanding that a company sitting at the crossroads of industrials and energy will probably see favorable tailwinds for several years. We could wait for a pullback, which could give us a 5% or 10% extra margin of safety. But, even considering where the stock is at right now, we are still in value territory. Tenaris continues to be a buy.

data.title: Commodities: Further U.S.-Iran Talks Set For This Week
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy, and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy - Some OPEC+ members keen to increase output

While oil prices edged higher on Friday, helped by a lower-than-expected US CPI print, ICE Brent still settled down week-on-week. However, there is still a large risk premium priced into the market given the uncertainty over how the situation between the US and Iran evolves. Comments from President Trump at the end of last week, saying that regime change would be best for Iran, will likely not ease concerns.

These comments come ahead of further US/Iran talks scheduled in Geneva for Tuesday. Meanwhile, the US will also be leading the Russia/Ukraine talks starting the same day in Geneva. A tone which is more de-escalatory should see the market start pricing in a smaller risk premium, which would allow more bearish oil fundamentals to take centre stage, driving oil prices lower.

There is growing noise around what OPEC+ may decide for April production levels when they meet on March 1. The group had suspended supply increases in the first quarter of this year due to seasonality. However, with our balance sheet continuing to show a large surplus in the second quarter, there is no need for the group to bring additional supply onto the market from April. Despite this, some OPEC+ members reportedly believe that the market can cope with additional supply increases. In our balance sheet, we are not assuming further increases from the group, so clearly, if further supply is brought onto the market, it will only lead to our surplus expectations growing.

There were only minor changes in speculative positioning over the week in ICE Brent. Speculators increased their net long by 2,813 lots to 281,062 lots as of last Tuesday. Lingering supply uncertainty when it comes to Iran, as well as Russian oil flows, has left speculators reluctant to sell the market.

Metals - US aluminium tariff rollback talk brings little relief

Reports that the US administration is considering rolling back part of its aluminium tariff regime did little to shift market sentiment. Any changes under discussion appear focused on derivative products rather than primary aluminium, meaning the core 50% levy stays in place. As a result, none of the structural pressures in the US market ease - domestic smelting capacity remains limited, dependence on Canadian supply unchanged, and the Midwest premium elevated after last year’s tariff hike.

Tariffs have already reshaped US trade flows, pushing primary metals away from the US and boosting scrap inflows, while some Canadian metal has been diverted to Europe. Even if derivative levies are eased, the current market dynamics won’t change much. And with exchange inventories effectively at zero, ongoing destocking and reports of large Q2 spot inquiries emerging, the physical market remains severely constrained.

Any rollback limited to derivatives wouldn’t feed through to LME pricing and would have only a modest impact on the Midwest premium. The broader picture is unchanged - global aluminium supply remains tight, inventories thin, speculative positioning elevated, and regional markets increasingly fragmented by policy risk.

Agriculture - Wheat falls on bumper output estimates

CBOT wheat prices came under pressure last week, with prices settling 1.8% lower on Friday, after India announced it would allow controlled wheat exports. There are reports that India, the second-largest wheat producer, approved exports of 2.5mt of wheat along with an additional 500kt of wheat products (wheat flour and semolina) following more than three years of export curbs. Additionally, improved crop estimates from Russia, Australia and France for the season have added further pressure on prices.

Recent numbers from France’s Agriculture Ministry show that 91% of the soft wheat crop is rated in good to excellent condition as of February 9, above 89% in the previous week and 73% in the same period last year. In contrast, 87% of durum wheat is rated in good to excellent condition, below the 93% seen the previous week but above 84% at the same stage last year.

The latest CFTC data shows that money managers decreased their net short in CBOT corn by 20,567 lots to 48,210 lots as of February 10. In contrast, speculators increased their net short in wheat by 3,900 lots to 85,655 lots. For soybeans, the net speculative long rose by 94,316 lots to 123,148 lots. This move was driven by both fresh longs entering the market and short covering amid expectations that China will boost purchases of US soybeans this season.

Positioning data in softs was more bearish. Speculators sold 24,415 lots of No.11 raw sugar to leave them with a net short of 211,789 lots - the largest short position held since 2019 - on the back of expectations of a large sugar surplus in the 2025/26 season. Meanwhile, despite the significant downward pressure we continue to see in cocoa prices, with London cocoa down almost 16% last week, the net short in London cocoa has not moved much over the last reporting week - speculators sold just 489 lots, leaving them with a net short of 24,468 lots.
